question_title: Will Houston Intercontinental Airport (IAH) record more than 2.00 inches of total precipitation during June 8–14, 2026?
summary: The climatological base rate for June 8-14 weekly precipitation exceeding 2.00 inches at IAH is approximately 21%, derived from Gamma distribution fitting (mean=1.26 in, median=0.62 in) and confirmed by multiple Monte Carlo approaches. The 2.00-inch threshold is above both the mean and median for this specific week. Current forecast signals are mixed: CPC outlooks favor above-normal precipitation for June overall and for the 6-10/8-14 day periods, but the NWS deterministic forecast shows a transition to a ridge-dominated drier regime starting exactly on June 8. These signals roughly offset with a slight net positive tilt, yielding a final estimate of ~24%. Key uncertainty stems from the inherent unpredictability of subtropical convective precipitation at 6-12 day lead times — a single organized thunderstorm event could produce 2+ inches in hours.

factors.description: CPC 30-day and 6-10 day outlooks favor above-normal precipitation for June 2026 and Jun 7-11 specifically. The 6-10 day outlook has high confidence (4/5) and directly covers the first part of the window. CFSv2 and ECMWF support the signal.
CPC 8-14 day outlook (Jun 9-15) shows slight tilt toward above-normal precipitation over southern Plains including Houston, with moderate confidence (3/5). Covers most of the target window.
NWS Area Forecast Discussion explicitly forecasts transition to ridge-dominated, hotter, drier regime starting June 8 — exactly when the window begins. QPF shows only trace amounts for Jun 8-10.
May 2026 very wet (8.84 in), high soil moisture, dewpoints 74-76°F maintaining strong Gulf moisture flux. Supports convective potential even under ridge conditions.
NWS daily PoPs for Jun 8-9 near normal (21-37%), not elevated. No organized weather systems or tropical disturbances currently targeting the area during the forecast window.

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary

**Question**: Will Houston IAH record >2.00 inches of precipitation during June 8–14, 2026?

**Probability: 0.24 (24%)**

### Key Reasoning

| Component | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Anchor** | 21% climatological base rate from Gamma fit to 1991-2020 normals (mean=1.26 in, median=0.62 in for Jun 8-14 week) |
| **Upward signals** | CPC 6-10 day outlook favors above-normal precip (confidence 4/5); CPC 30-day outlook above-normal for June; high soil moisture and Gulf moisture flux |
| **Downward signals** | NWS AFD forecasts ridge-dominated regime starting Jun 8; QPF shows only trace amounts; daily PoPs near normal |
| **Net adjustment** | +3pp from base rate (mixed signals with slight positive tilt) |

### Distribution modeling
- **Gamma fit** (shape=0.528): P(>2.00) = 20.8%
- **Daily exponential MC**: P(>2.00) = 21.0%
- **Regime-switching MC**: P(>2.00) = 23.8%
- **Scenario-weighted**: P(>2.00) = 21.1%

All approaches converge on the low-to-mid 20s, with the threshold sitting above both the mean (1.26 in) and median (0.62 in) of the weekly distribution. Houston's convective rainfall variability means a single heavy thunderstorm event could resolve YES, but the probability of such an event during this specific 7-day window under expected ridge conditions is limited.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will Houston Intercontinental Airport (IAH) record more than 2.00 inches of total precipitation during June 8–14, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43753)
resolution_criteria:
This question resolves YES if the sum of the daily precipitation values ("PCPN" column) at Houston Intercontinental Airport (IAH) for the seven calendar days June 8, 2026 through June 14, 2026 (inclusive, local station date) exceeds 2.00 inches, as reported in the NWS Preliminary Local Climatological Data (CF6) product for IAH: https://forecast.weather.gov/product.php?site=NWS&product=CF6&issuedby=IAH

A daily "T" (trace) entry counts as 0.00 inches. If the CF6 product is unavailable, the equivalent daily totals from the NWS NOWData tool for the Houston Intercontinental Airport station (https://www.weather.gov/wrh/climate?wfo=hgx) or from credible sources (https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) reporting official NWS IAH daily precipitation observations will be used.
fine_print: If a daily value is initially missing ("M") and later filled in via the certified NCEI record, the certified value will be used. If a value is still missing for one or more days when the question is resolved, the question resolves based on the best available official NWS/NCEI observation; if total measurable precipitation already exceeds 2.00 inches on the non-missing days, it resolves YES regardless.

factors.description: Climatological base rate: Week 2 of June at IAH historically averages 1.26 inches (mean) with median 0.62 inches. Gamma distribution fit gives P(>2.00) = 20.8%. Multiple simulation approaches confirm 21-24%.
CPC 30-day and 6-10 day outlooks favor above-normal precipitation for June and Jun 7-11 respectively. The 6-10 day outlook has high confidence (4/5) and directly covers the first days of the window.
CPC 8-14 day outlook (Jun 9-15) shows slight tilt toward above-normal precipitation over southern Plains including Houston, with moderate confidence (3/5).
NWS Area Forecast Discussion explicitly forecasts transition to ridge-dominated, hotter, drier regime starting June 8 - exactly when our window begins. QPF shows only trace amounts.
May 2026 very wet (8.84 inches), high soil moisture, dewpoints 74-76°F maintaining Gulf moisture flux - supports convective potential even under ridge.
NWS daily PoPs for Jun 8-9 near normal (21-37%), not elevated. No organized weather systems or tropical disturbances targeting the area.

input.anchor: Climatological base rate: 21% (P(Jun 8-14 weekly precip > 2.00 inches) from Gamma distribution fit to 1991-2020 normals for IAH: mean=1.26 in, median=0.62 in, shape=0.528)
input.assessment:
The question asks whether Houston IAH will record >2.00 inches during June 8-14, 2026. This is a threshold question on a continuous precipitation variable.

The climatological base rate is solidly ~21% based on multiple simulation approaches (Gamma fit, daily model, regime-switching model). The threshold of 2.00 inches over 7 days is above the mean (1.26 in) and well above the median (0.62 in) for this specific week.

Current forecast signals are mixed: CPC outlooks favor above-normal precipitation for June overall and for the 6-10/8-14 day periods, but the NWS deterministic forecast shows a transition to ridge dominance starting exactly on June 8. The active weather pattern (upper-level low) is expected to drive rain June 5-7, with the ridge building afterward.

The positive CPC signal and negative NWS ridge signal roughly offset each other, though the CPC outlook covers a wider probability envelope. I estimate a small net positive adjustment of ~2-3 percentage points above the pure climatological rate, landing at approximately 24%.

Key uncertainty: at 6-12 days out, weather forecasts have limited deterministic skill for precipitation in subtropical environments dominated by convective processes. A single organized thunderstorm complex could easily produce 2+ inches in one day.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor is 21% from fitted Gamma distribution. My estimate of 24% is only slightly above the anchor, reflecting the mixed but marginally positive forecast signals. The 2.00-inch threshold is above both the mean and median, making YES the less likely outcome. No hedging toward 50% - the evidence supports a probability in the low-to-mid 20s.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether the ridge forecast for Jun 8+ materializes as expected or if the active pattern lingers. 2. Convective precipitation is inherently chaotic - a single MCS could dump 2+ inches in hours. 3. Skill of 6-12 day deterministic precipitation forecasts is very low for point locations in convective environments.
input.tool_audit: research() returned excellent data on: historical climate normals (mean, median, percentiles for Jun 8-14 week), current NWS forecast details (QPF, PoP, AFD), CPC outlooks (6-10, 8-14 day, monthly, seasonal). All three research queries succeeded. Code execution worked well for Gamma fitting and Monte Carlo simulations. No tool failures.
input.update_triggers: 1. Updated NWS/GFS/ECMWF model runs showing organized precip systems targeting Houston Jun 8-14. 2. A tropical disturbance forming in the Gulf. 3. Updated CPC outlooks shifting the precipitation signal. 4. Ridge pattern verification/failure in early June.
input.process_reflection: The research tool provided exceptionally thorough data from NOAA ACIS, NWS API, CPC outlooks - exactly what was needed. The scaffolding worked well for this precipitation threshold question. The main friction was that I couldn't directly access historical daily data to count exceedances empirically; I had to rely on distributional fitting from summary statistics. A tool that directly queried NOAA ACIS or NCEI for station-level daily data would have been valuable for ground-truthing the Gamma fit.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

### Hallucinated evidence — None found
All six factors are fully grounded in the research trace:
- **Factor 1** (climatological base rate: mean=1.26, median=0.62, Gamma P(>2.00)=20.8%): Confirmed by research entry showing Week 2 (Jun 8-14) averages 1.26 in with median 0.62 in, and by four code execution blocks computing the Gamma fit (shape=0.528, scale=2.387) yielding P(>2.00)=20.84%, confirmed by Monte Carlo at 20.9%.
- **Factor 2** (CPC 6-10 day above-normal, confidence 4/5): Confirmed in research: "CPC 6-10 Day Outlook (Jun 7-11) favors above-normal precipitation for Houston area due to Gulf moisture inflow; confidence 4/5."
- **Factor 3** (CPC 8-14 day slight above-normal, confidence 3/5): Confirmed: "CPC 8-14 Day Outlook (Jun 9-15) shows slight tilt toward above-normal precipitation over southern Plains including Houston, but with significant uncertainty; confidence 3/5."
- **Factor 4** (NWS AFD ridge-dominated regime starting June 8, QPF trace): Confirmed: "transition to ridge regime Jun 8+" and "NWS QPF shows only trace precipitation (~0.02 inches) for June 8."
- **Factor 5** (May 2026 8.84 inches, dewpoints 74-76°F): Confirmed in question background and research: "May 2026 at IAH ended with 8.84 inches" and "Dewpoints of 74-76°F."
- **Factor 6** (PoPs 21-37%): Confirmed: "Precipitation probability for June 8 is 27-37% (CPC/NWS), dropping to 21-23% on June 9."

### Double-counting — Mild (non-impactful)
Factors 2 and 3 both cite CPC outlooks covering overlapping time windows (Jun 7-11 and Jun 9-15). These are technically separate products but draw on related ensemble model data. However, the assigned logits are small (+0.3 and +0.2) and the confidence levels are moderate (0.6 and 0.5), so the effective impact is minimal. Similarly, Factor 6 (PoPs near normal) partially derives from the same NWS forecast data underlying Factor 4 (ridge regime). Again, the logits are small enough that this doesn't materially distort the forecast.

### Wrong-direction factors — None found
All logit signs are correct: positive for above-normal signals (Factors 2, 3, 5), negative for drier/ridge signals (Factors 4, 6), and neutral for the base rate (Factor 1).

### Contradictory assessment — None found
The assessment concludes ~24%, which is the 21% base rate + ~3pp net positive adjustment. This is consistent with the factors: the positive CPC signals and negative ridge signals roughly offset with a slight net positive tilt. The agent explicitly acknowledges this in the thinking section.

### Missing resolution criteria — Not an issue
Resolution criteria are fully provided and the agent correctly engages with the specific CF6 product, PCPN column, IAH station, and the June 8-14 date range.

### Resolution misalignment — None found
The agent's simulation targets the exact quantity (sum of 7 daily precipitation values exceeding 2.00 inches) specified in the resolution criteria.

### Counterargument quality — Good
The counterargument substantively argues the probability should be higher (30-40%), constructing a coherent alternative story: CPC outlooks are more informative than NWS deterministic at Day 6+, ridges don't suppress Houston convection given 74-76°F dewpoints, single heavy rain events can resolve YES by themselves, and the Gamma fit may underestimate Houston's heavy precipitation tail. This is a genuine alternative argument, not a token gesture.

### Confidence assessment — Appropriate
0.45 confidence is well-calibrated for a 6-12 day precipitation forecast in a subtropical convective environment with mixed forecast signals.

### Probability assessment — Reasonable
The 24% estimate represents a modest +3pp departure from the well-grounded 21% climatological anchor. The Gamma fit, daily model, and regime-switching model all converge on 21-24% as the base rate. The scenario-weighted analysis incorporating forecast signals gives 21.1%. The agent's final 24% reflects a small upward adjustment for the CPC above-normal signal. My independent estimate based on the same evidence would be in the 22-26% range. The agent's 24% is squarely within this range and well-justified.

### Anchor divergence — Minimal
Anchor is 21%, final is 24% — only a 3pp departure, well within the range justified by the above-normal CPC signals.

### Tool audit — Consistent
Agent claims "No tool failures." Ground truth confirms 7 calls, 0 errors (0.0% error rate). No discrepancy.

### Cross-question/worldview consistency — No issues
No existing numeric sub-forecast for IAH weekly precipitation was found in the worldview store. The three worldview research entries are the same entries cited in the trace and are consistent with the factor claims.
